facade_colour: pink painted brick facade with extensive graffiti art on roll-down shutters

hcd_data.architectural_style: Converted House-form, Detached (confirmed – HCD Plan Vol. 2)
hcd_data.statement_of_contribution: The property has value as a nineteenth-century House-form building that has been modified to accommodate a commercial conversion through a one-storey commercial addition. Through its construction date and typology, the property is associated with the District’s early residential development from the 1850s to the early 1900s. With its visibly layered built form, the property is also associated with the patterns of commercial adaptation and expansion that began in the early twentieth century. The property retains building features reflecting vernacular interpretations of Victorian architectural styles, including its gabled ended roof with finial and decorative bargeboard, lancet arch attic window, and two segmentally arched windows at the second storey. Contextually, the property is important in maintaining and supporting the historic mixed use character of the area and is physically, functionally, visually, and historically linked to the surrounding market area.

context.street_character: One-way NB | 30 km/h | Sub-standard sidewalks | Primary commercial spine | Pedestrian Sunday route

facade_description: 214 Augusta Ave is a converted house-form, detached building. The street elevation spans roughly 5.2 metres across 1 storeys and is capped by a gable, consistent with its converted house-form, detached typology. The facade reads primarily as brick, laid in running bond with light grey mortar joints. The facade keeps a domestic opening rhythm of 0 window bays from lower to upper levels, using storefront, and 1 principal entrance.
heritage_summary: The property retains building features reflecting vernacular interpretations of Victorian architectural styles, including its gabled ended roof with finial and decorative bargeboard, lancet arch attic window, and two segmentally arched windows at the second storey. With its visibly layered built form, the property is also associated with the patterns of commercial adaptation and expansion that began in the early twentieth century.
